food_subgroup: Starchy root desserts in coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet tapioca in coconut milk; closest_parent is the general cassava food product class (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cassava and products as a category; no specific code exists for the prepared sweet coconut milk dessert form.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sweet tapioca in coconut milk as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one small bowl of sweet tapioca pieces in coconut milk sauce as typically served in Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Cassava (tapioca) boiled in coconut milk with sugar — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Cassava raw (FDC ID 169985) and Coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), with sugar addition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai sweet tapioca in coconut milk as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: boiled cassava (FDC ID 169985), coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and added sugar, combined in proportions typical of the dish (approximately 55% cassava, 35% coconut milk, 10% sugar by weight after cooking). Energy and macros are calculated from these proportions. Secondary reference: FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for cassava-based desserts.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Cassava boiled (analogous estimate for sweet tapioca in coconut milk)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai sweet tapioca in coconut milk. The GI of 46 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled cassava (GI approximately 46, low category). Coconut milk fat content is expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled cassava. The estimate is conservative and uses the boiled cassava value as the primary anchor. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (46 × 49.6) / 100 ≈ 22.8, rounded to 22.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g; ~110 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Sweet tapioca in coconut milk contains moderate sodium (~110 mg per 200 g serving), which is within acceptable limits for hypertensive individuals when consumed occasionally, but portion control is advised if salt is added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate and sugar content with moderate glycemic load (~22.9 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 49.6 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~22.9 per 200 g serving, sweet tapioca in coconut milk should be consumed in small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, and ideally paired with a protein or fibre source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5 g per 100 g; ~9 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (~9 g per 200 g serving) is clinically relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be limited to occasional servings and portion size kept small to manage saturated fat intake within daily targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Tapioca in Coconut Milk (椰奶甜木薯; Thai: มันสำปะหลังหวานในกะทิ) is a traditional Thai dessert made from chunks of boiled cassava root (tapioca) simmered in sweetened coconut milk, often flavoured with pandan leaf and a pinch of salt. The cassava pieces are soft and slightly chewy with a mild starchy flavour, while the coconut milk sauce is rich, creamy, and gently sweet. The dish is served warm or at room temperature and is a common street food and restaurant dessert across Thailand and in Thai restaurants throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dessert (~148 kcal per 100 g) with a high carbohydrate content (~24.8 g per 100 g) and notable saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 46 (low category) based on boiled cassava data, though the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 22.9, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. It is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content, and portion control is recommended for those with diabetes or hypertension.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai sweet tapioca in coconut milk as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for boiled cassava and coconut milk combined in typical proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled cassava as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (boiled cassava FDC ID 169985; coconut milk FDC ID 170172) and added sugar, combined in proportions typical of the dish (~55% cassava, ~35% coconut milk, ~10% sugar by cooked weight). No single approved-source entry for this prepared dish was found. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component sources used. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component source for this preparation. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component source for this preparation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component source for this preparation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component source for this preparation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as cassava and coconut milk contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as neither cassava nor coconut milk contains meaningful vitamin D. 11. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate based on boiled cassava from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this prepared dish was found. 12. The food_name in the input ('803D Sweet Tapioca 椰奶甜木薯') contains a menu code prefix ('803D') which has been removed to produce the canonical English food name 'Sweet Tapioca in Coconut Milk'. The Chinese name 椰奶甜木薯 from the input has been preserved. 13. This is a Thai cuisine dish; mandatory Thai-script local name has been provided. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not mandatory for Thai cuisine per G6 rules and have been left null. 14. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology term exists for this prepared dessert.
